# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

4	/// Rasterises the full menu bar label into a non-template `NSImage`.
5	/// MenuBarExtra's label was unusable: template tinting stripped the per-tier
6	/// dot colors, and mixed HStacks silently truncated segments past the first.
7	/// With a custom `NSStatusItem`, we control `isTemplate = false` so hues
8	/// survive and we pick the text color from the status button's effective
9	/// appearance (which tracks the menu bar, not the app).
10	@MainActor
11	enum MenuBarLabelRenderer {
12	    private static let font: NSFont = .menuBarFont(ofSize: 0)
13	    private static let dot = "● "
14	    private static let horizontalPadding: CGFloat = 4
15	    private static let height: CGFloat = 18
16	    static let unconfiguredCallToAction = "Configure AI Metrics"
17	
18	    static func render(
19	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
20	        separator: String,
21	        fallbackText: String,
22	        isDarkMenuBar: Bool,
23	        isUnconfigured: Bool = false
24	    ) -> NSImage {
25	        let textColor: NSColor = isDarkMenuBar ? .white : .black
26	        let attributed: NSAttributedString
27	        if isUnconfigured && segments.isEmpty {
28	            attributed = unconfiguredAttributedString(textColor: textColor)
29	        } else {
30	            attributed = attributedString(
31	                segments: segments,
32	                separator: separator,
33	                fallbackText: fallbackText,
34	                textColor: textColor
35	            )
36	        }
37	        let textSize = attributed.size()
38	        let width = ceil(textSize.width) + horizontalPadding * 2
39	        let size = NSSize(width: max(width, 1), height: height)
40	        let image = NSImage(size: size)
41	        image.lockFocus()
42	        let textY = (height - ceil(textSize.height)) / 2
43	        attributed.draw(at: NSPoint(x: horizontalPadding, y: textY))
44	        image.unlockFocus()
45	        image.isTemplate = false
46	        return image
47	    }
48	
49	    private static func unconfiguredAttributedString(textColor: NSColor) -> NSAttributedString {
50	        let result = NSMutableAttributedString()
51	        let gear = NSImage(systemSymbolName: "gearshape", accessibilityDescription: nil)
52	        if let gear {
53	            let configured = gear.withSymbolConfiguration(
54	                NSImage.SymbolConfiguration(pointSize: font.pointSize, weight: .regular)
55	            ) ?? gear
56	            configured.isTemplate = true
57	            let tinted = NSImage(size: configured.size, flipped: false) { rect in
58	                configured.draw(in: rect)
59	                textColor.set()
60	                rect.fill(using: .sourceAtop)
61	                return true
62	            }
63	            tinted.isTemplate = false
64	            let attachment = NSTextAttachment()
65	            attachment.image = tinted
66	            attachment.bounds = CGRect(
67	                x: 0,
68	                y: -1,
69	                width: tinted.size.width,
70	                height: tinted.size.height
71	            )
72	            result.append(NSAttributedString(attachment: attachment))
73	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
74	                string: " ",
75	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
76	            ))
77	        }
78	        result.append(NSAttributedString(
79	            string: unconfiguredCallToAction,
80	            attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
81	        ))
82	        return result
83	    }
84	
85	    private static func attributedString(
86	        segments: [MenuBarSegment],
87	        separator: String,
88	        fallbackText: String,
89	        textColor: NSColor
90	    ) -> NSAttributedString {
91	        guard !segments.isEmpty else {
92	            return NSAttributedString(
93	                string: fallbackText,
94	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
95	            )
96	        }
97	        let result = NSMutableAttributedString()
98	        for (index, segment) in segments.enumerated() {
99	            if index > 0, !separator.isEmpty {
100	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
101	                    string: separator,
102	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
103	                ))
104	            }
105	            if let vendor = segment.vendorIcon,
106	               let icon = VendorBranding.tintedNSImage(for: vendor, height: font.capHeight) {
107	                let attachment = NSTextAttachment()
108	                attachment.image = icon
109	                let pad = VendorBranding.iconGlowPadding
110	                attachment.bounds = CGRect(
111	                    x: -pad,
112	                    y: -pad,
113	                    width: icon.size.width,
114	                    height: icon.size.height
115	                )
116	                result.append(NSAttributedString(attachment: attachment))
117	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
118	                    string: "\u{2009}",
119	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
120	                ))
121	            }
122	            if segment.showDot {
123	                let dotColor = segment.tier?.nsColor ?? textColor
124	                result.append(NSAttributedString(
125	                    string: dot,
126	                    attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: dotColor]
127	                ))
128	            }
129	            result.append(NSAttributedString(
130	                string: segment.text,
131	                attributes: [.font: font, .foregroundColor: textColor]
132	            ))
133	        }
134	        return result
135	    }
136	}

5	enum VendorBranding {
6	    struct Brand: Equatable, Sendable {
7	        let assetName: String
8	        let displayName: String
9	        let tintHex: String
10	    }
11	
12	    static func brand(for vendor: Vendor) -> Brand? {
13	        switch vendor {
14	        case .claude:
15	            Brand(
16	                assetName: "claude-mark",
17	                displayName: "Claude Code",
18	                tintHex: "DA7756"
19	            )
20	        case .codex:
21	            Brand(
22	                assetName: "codex-mark",
23	                displayName: "Codex",
24	                tintHex: "10A37F"
25	            )
26	        default:
27	            nil
28	        }
29	    }
30	
31	    static func displayName(for vendor: Vendor) -> String {
32	        brand(for: vendor)?.displayName ?? vendor.rawValue
33	    }
34	
35	    static func tint(for vendor: Vendor) -> Color? {
36	        guard let tintHex = brand(for: vendor)?.tintHex else { return nil }
37	        return Color(hex: tintHex)
38	    }
39	
40	    static func icon(for vendor: Vendor) -> NSImage? {
41	        guard let assetName = brand(for: vendor)?.assetName else { return nil }
42	        return loadTemplateImage(named: assetName)
43	    }
44	
45	    /// Pixels of white halo added on each side around the vendor icon for legibility.
46	    static let iconGlowPadding: CGFloat = 3.0
47	
48	    /// Returns a tinted, non-template copy of the vendor icon at the requested height.
49	    /// Uses the brand's `tintHex` as fill color and the template PDF as an alpha mask.
50	    /// A crisp white halo is produced by drawing a white silhouette at pixel offsets
51	    /// in all directions — no blur, so the icon stays sharp on any menu bar tint color.
52	    @MainActor
53	    static func tintedNSImage(for vendor: Vendor, height: CGFloat) -> NSImage? {
54	        guard let template = icon(for: vendor),
55	              let brand = brand(for: vendor),
56	              let tintColor = NSColor(hex: brand.tintHex) else { return nil }
57	        let aspectRatio = template.size.height > 0
58	            ? template.size.width / template.size.height : 1
59	        let iconSize = NSSize(width: ceil(height * aspectRatio), height: ceil(height))
60	        let pad = Self.iconGlowPadding
61	        let canvasSize = NSSize(width: iconSize.width + pad * 2, height: iconSize.height + pad * 2)
62	        let iconRect = NSRect(x: pad, y: pad, width: iconSize.width, height: iconSize.height)
63	
64	        let tinted = NSImage(size: iconSize)
65	        tinted.lockFocus()
66	        tintColor.setFill()
67	        NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize).fill()
68	        template.draw(
69	            in: NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize),
70	            from: .zero,
71	            operation: .destinationIn,
72	            fraction: 1.0
73	        )
74	        tinted.unlockFocus()
75	
76	        let white = NSImage(size: iconSize)
77	        white.lockFocus()
78	        NSColor.white.setFill()
79	        NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize).fill()
80	        template.draw(
81	            in: NSRect(origin: .zero, size: iconSize),
82	            from: .zero,
83	            operation: .destinationIn,
84	            fraction: 1.0
85	        )
86	        white.unlockFocus()
87	
88	        let result = NSImage(size: canvasSize)
89	        result.lockFocus()
90	        // Draw the white silhouette at every surrounding pixel offset to build a crisp halo.
91	        for dx: CGFloat in [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2] {
92	            for dy: CGFloat in [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2] where !(dx == 0 && dy == 0) {
93	                white.draw(in: NSRect(
94	                    x: iconRect.minX + dx, y: iconRect.minY + dy,
95	                    width: iconRect.width, height: iconRect.height
96	                ))
97	            }
98	        }
99	        tinted.draw(in: iconRect)
100	        result.unlockFocus()
101	        result.isTemplate = false
102	        return result
103	    }
104	
105	    /// Resolves the SwiftPM resource bundle without ever touching `Bundle.module`,
106	    /// whose synthesized accessor traps with a precondition failure when the
107	    /// expected `.bundle` is missing next to the executable. We probe every
108	    /// location the bundle may legitimately land in across `swift run`,
109	    /// `swift build`, the assembled `.app`, and merged-into-main-bundle layouts —
110	    /// returning `nil` instead of crashing the app when none match.
111	    private static let resourceBundleName = "AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers"
112	
113	    private static let resourceBundle: Bundle? = {
114	        let bundleFile = "\(resourceBundleName).bundle"
115	        let mainURL = Bundle.main.bundleURL
116	        let tokenBundle = Bundle(for: BundleToken.self)
117	        let candidates: [URL?] = [
118	            Bundle.main.resourceURL,
119	            mainURL,
120	            mainURL.appendingPathComponent("Contents/MacOS"),
121	            mainURL.appendingPathComponent("Contents/Resources"),
122	            tokenBundle.resourceURL,
123	            tokenBundle.bundleURL,
124	        ]
125	        for candidate in candidates.compactMap({ $0 }) {
126	            let bundleURL = candidate.appendingPathComponent(bundleFile)
127	            if let bundle = Bundle(url: bundleURL) {
128	                return bundle
129	            }
130	        }
131	        return nil
132	    }()
133	
134	    private static func loadTemplateImage(named assetName: String) -> NSImage? {
135	        let url = locateAsset(named: assetName, fileExtension: "pdf", subdirectory: "VendorBranding")
136	        guard let url, let image = NSImage(contentsOf: url) else { return nil }
137	        image.isTemplate = true
138	        return image
139	    }
140	
141	    private static func locateAsset(
142	        named assetName: String,
143	        fileExtension: String,
144	        subdirectory: String
145	    ) -> URL? {
146	        if let url = Bundle.main.url(
147	            forResource: assetName,
148	            withExtension: fileExtension,
149	            subdirectory: subdirectory
150	        ) {
151	            return url
152	        }
153	        if let url = resourceBundle?.url(
154	            forResource: assetName,
155	            withExtension: fileExtension,
156	            subdirectory: subdirectory
157	        ) {
158	            return url
159	        }
160	        return nil
161	    }
162	}

Now let me look at `UsageDetailsView.swift` more carefully to understand the full context of the `emptyState` view. In particular, I want to understand how the `emptyState` is invoked and what the state conditions are.

The key issue I want to look at:

1. In `MenuBarLabelRenderer.swift`, there's a condition `if isUnconfigured && segments.isEmpty` (line 27). The `isUnconfigured` is passed from `AppDelegate` as `Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSegments.isEmpty`. So `isUnconfigured` is always true when `segments.isEmpty`. 

I'm noticing that `segments` comes from `store.menuBarSegments` while `isUnconfigured` is based on `Self.sharedPreferences.menuBarSegments`, so they could theoretically diverge—though in practice, if the preferences are empty, the store's computed segments should be empty too.

Looking at the logic more carefully, this distinction seems intentional: when preferences are unconfigured, show the setup prompt; when they're configured but data hasn't loaded yet, fall through to the normal empty state handling.

Now I'm examining how `VendorBranding.swift` locates assets—it tries multiple bundle candidates but I'm tracing through the exact order of attempts in `locateAsset` to see if `Bundle.main` is checked first.

Checking the gear image configuration...

The symbol configuration applies a point size and weight, and if it fails to apply, the code falls back to the original gear image with template mode enabled. The tinted image is then created using the configured size, which should work correctly even if symbol configuration doesn't succeed on macOS.

I'm noticing a potential issue in how `unconfiguredAttributedString` handles the `configured` image — setting `isTemplate = true` on an NSImage returned from `withSymbolConfiguration` could modify a shared system object, though this is unlikely since system images aren't typically cached.

Looking at `UsageDetailsView.swift`'s `emptyState`, it directly accesses `AppDelegate.sharedPreferences.menuBarSegments.isEmpty` as a computed property without using SwiftUI's state observation mechanisms, so the view won't reactively update when preferences change — though this pattern appears consistent with the rest of the codebase.

Now I have a full picture. Let me identify actual bugs:

**Bug candidate 1: `MenuBarLabelRenderer` - `isUnconfigured` condition is logically redundant but not incorrect**

The `isUnconfigured && segments.isEmpty` check in `MenuBarLabelRenderer.render` - since both `isUnconfigured` and `segments.isEmpty` derive from the same `sharedPreferences`, one is always equal to the other. The `&& segments.isEmpty` part is dead code. However, this could be argued as defensive coding, not a bug.

**Bug candidate 2: `VendorBranding.resourceBundle` - uses `Bundle(url:)` which returns nil if the path doesn't exist**


Actually, I need to reconsider this. The code iterates through candidate paths and tries to initialize a Bundle at each URL, but `Bundle(url:)` on macOS doesn't validate whether the URL actually exists—it just creates a Bundle object. The real validation happens later when you try to access resources from it, so this isn't necessarily a bug either, just potentially inefficient.

Let me move on to examining the next issue in the code. Looking more carefully at how `@Observable` works with static property access — the macro generates observation tracking that registers property accesses with SwiftUI's observation context, so even accessing `AppDelegate.sharedPreferences` statically will properly trigger view updates when those properties change.

For the `VendorIconView` fallback, showing initials when the bundle is missing is intentional, and unknown vendors correctly return nil without attempting to load anything, so that's working as designed.

Now examining the hardcoded metric names in `UsageStore.makeDefaultPreferences()`...

these strings were previously in `MenuBarSegmentsSeeder.defaultSegments()` and are now in the usage store. They're configuration keys that need to match metric names defined elsewhere, so extracting them as named constants would align with the Swift guidelines, though this is pre-existing behavior that's just been relocated. The real question is whether these strings are actually being tested in their new location or if they're only used by test code.

Now I'm looking at a potential issue in `MenuBarLabelRenderer.unconfiguredAttributedString` where an NSImage is being created with a closure that captures `configured` and `textColor`. When the closure draws the configured image...

Actually, I think the real problem is about how template images behave in different rendering contexts. Setting `isTemplate = true` affects how the system tints the image, but that tinting only applies when drawing into a view context. Here, we're drawing into an NSImage closure, which is a different rendering context entirely, so the template tinting behavior might not work as expected. So the `textColor.set()` and `.sourceAtop` blend mode ensure the final color is applied correctly, regardless of the template property. The `isTemplate` flag mainly affects automatic tinting in UI contexts like buttons or status items, but here we're explicitly controlling the color in the drawing handler, so it doesn't impact the result. The tinted gear icon comes out with the right color.

Now I'm looking at what's actually broken in this PR. The key changes are that `MenuBarSegmentsSeeder.defaultSegments()` now returns an empty array instead of the S+W segments, those segments moved into `UsageStore.makeDefaultPreferences()`, and `VendorBranding` has some defensive logic added.

There are also updates to `MenuBarLabelRenderer` with a new unconfigured state display, `AppDelegate.refreshStatusItemImage()` now passes an `isUnconfigured` flag, and `UsageDetailsView.emptyState` has a corresponding unconfigured view.

I'm noticing a potential issue with the bundle resolver—when `Bundle(url:)` creates a Bundle object, it doesn't automatically load the bundle's Info.plist. For `url(forResource:withExtension:)` to work properly, the bundle needs to be loaded first. In practice, calling `url(forResource:...)` does trigger lazy loading, so this should work, but it's worth noting.

Looking at the asset location logic, in the `.app` case the PDF assets live in `AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers.bundle`, not directly in `Bundle.main`, so the first lookup would fail and the fallback to `resourceBundle` would find them correctly. The dual-check approach handles both scenarios—when resources are copied directly to `Bundle.main` and when they're in a separate bundle.

Examining the test changes, `MenuBarSegmentsSeederTests` removed a few tests including one that verified seeded segments target the currently-active Claude account.

For `swift run` though, the situation is different—`Bundle.main` points to the executable itself, which doesn't have a traditional bundle structure, so `resourceURL` might be nil or point to the executable's directory rather than where the resource bundle actually lives at `.build/debug/AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers.bundle`. I'm realizing that for a bare command-line tool built with SwiftPM, `Bundle.main.bundleURL` and `resourceURL` both point to the executable's directory since there's no app bundle structure with a Contents/Resources subdirectory. So when `swift run` executes the binary at `.build/debug/AIUsagesTrackers`, both bundle properties reference that same directory path.

The actual SwiftPM resource bundle sits at `.build/debug/AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers.bundle` in the same directory, but my current candidates for finding it—using either `Bundle.main.resourceURL` or `mainURL`—would both look inside the executable itself rather than alongside it, which is the wrong location. I need to reconsider whether `Bundle.main.bundleURL` for a bare executable actually points to the executable file itself or to its parent directory. So the bundle probing works as expected—candidate[0] successfully locates the bundle file. The main issue I found is that misleading test comment, which I should address in my diff. Now I'm checking the gear icon tinting logic in `unconfiguredAttributedString`, where an image drawing handler captures the configured image and text color to apply a tint effect when the image is rendered.

The key insight is that while the drawing closure is lazy, it gets triggered when the attributed string is drawn during the menu bar refresh cycle, so the text color is captured at the right moment for each render pass.

Now I'm checking the `VendorBranding.resourceBundle` initialization—it's a static property that gets set up once, and after that first access it either holds a valid Bundle or remains nil.  If the SwiftPM bundle isn't available yet during testing, it'll cache nil and never retry, but in practice the bundle should be ready by the time icon loading happens.

Looking at the `UsageStore.makeDefaultPreferences()` method, there's a comment explaining why the old defaults are kept inline rather than moved elsewhere—it's to allow onboarding to ship an empty seed without breaking tests and callers.

But here's the issue: when that behavior was moved from `MenuBarSegmentsSeeder` into `makeDefaultPreferences()`, the test that verified it was removed without adding a new test for the method itself. So the S+W segment defaults are now untested, and if someone changes them, nothing would catch it. This does impact maintainability, though the codebase doesn't seem to have comprehensive test coverage everywhere, so it's borderline whether this is worth flagging.

Looking more closely, the removed tests were specifically checking those S+W defaults, and now that the defaults live in `makeDefaultPreferences()` with no dedicated test, a future developer could modify them without CI catching it. Though I'm wondering if `makeDefaultPreferences()` even needs its own tests since it's private and only used as a fallback when no preferences are provided—the defaults aren't part of the onboarding flow anymore anyway. The S+W defaults in there serve a different purpose now.

The real gap to investigate is whether any existing test actually exercises `UsageStore` when initialized without explicit preferences, which would implicitly validate those defaults.

Since `makeDefaultPreferences()` is private, the testing guidelines don't require direct coverage, and the behavior it controls is an implementation detail rather than a public contract. The only concrete issue worth flagging is that misleading test comment, so I'll go ahead and post the DiffComment.

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

--- Changes ---

9c8e56d docs: document the first-launch onboarding flow in README
Walks new users through what they see right after install: the
"Configure AI Metrics" menu bar prompt, where to click to add
the first segment, and what to expect once configured.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: ebc0a04a52c0

---
b6112d8 fix(app): prevent Bundle.module crash and improve first-launch onboarding
Two interrelated issues fixed:

1. Bundle.module crash on Homebrew install when resource bundle is missing
   next to the executable. Introduced a defensive resolver that probes
   multiple known bundle locations (Bundle.main.resourceURL, Contents/MacOS/,
   Contents/Resources/, etc.) and returns nil gracefully instead of
   trapping. VendorIconView falls back to the vendor's first letter when
   the asset is absent, keeping the UI readable instead of blank.

2. Onboarding assumes existing Claude usage: MenuBarSegmentsSeeder now
   returns an empty array so the app invites configuration instead of
   pre-seeding segments. Menu bar label renders "Configure AI Metrics"
   with a gear icon when unconfigured. UsageDetailsView popover shows
   a clear CTA ("Open Settings") guiding the user to add their first segment.
   UsageStore.makeDefaultPreferences() preserves the S+W defaults for
   backward compatibility in tests and callers that don't supply preferences.

Tests updated: MenuBarSegmentsSeederTests checks for empty defaults;
new VendorBranding test confirms the icon lookup returns nil instead of
crashing for unknown vendors.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 5539b2a88ce3

---
ad3ad63 docs: limit screenshot heights to improve README layout
Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: a24b8e70365a

---
5c70139 fix(build-app-bundle): include SwiftPM resource bundles in app bundle
SwiftPM emits resource bundles (AIUsagesTrackers_AIUsagesTrackers.bundle)
next to the executable when targets declare `resources:`. The build script
was not copying these into the .app bundle, causing Bundle.module to
fatal-error on first access (crash before applicationDidFinishLaunching
completed, so seeding never ran).

Now copies all *.bundle files from the build directory alongside the binary
in Contents/MacOS/, where Bundle.module looks for them. Also generates a
minimal Info.plist in each bundle so codesign --deep accepts the flat bundle
format and can sign the parent app without rejecting embedded bundles.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 222a6ed1e3c2
